claims.claim: Decomposition of LiPF6-containing electrolytes under thermal heating conditions causes emissions of toxic gases hydrogen fluoride (HF) and phosphoryl fluoride (POF3), where POF3 can be considered a precursor of HF in humid environments.
claims.quote: Decomposition of LiPF6-containing electrolytes under thermal heating conditions has been found to cause emissions of toxic gases such as hydrogen ﬂuoride, HF, and phosphoryl ﬂuoride, POF3, which, in humid environments, can be considered a precursor of HF

claims.claim: Non-cyclic carbonates (DMC, DEC, EA) evaporate at temperatures below 100°C, while cyclic carbonates (EC and PC) have negligible vapor pressure at low temperature and evaporate at temperatures above 100°C.
claims.quote: the non-cyclic carbonates are more volatile and will evaporate at temperatures below 100 (cid:3)C, while the cyclic carbonates (EC and PC) have negligible vapor pressure at low temperature and evaporate at temperatures above 100 (cid:3)C

claims.claim: Emissions of HF and POF3 were simultaneously detected from 100°C to 170°C during thermal heating of extracted Li-ion battery electrolyte containing LiPF6.
claims.quote: Regarding the decomposition of LiPF6, emissions of HF and one of its precursors, POF3, have been distinctly and almost simultaneously detected in the TG/FT-IR analysis from 100 (cid:3)C to 170 (cid:3)C

claims.claim: The simultaneous appearance and decrease of HF and POF3 emissions following a similar pattern may be explained by insufficient residual humidity to convert POF3 into HF.
claims.quote: The fact that the emissions of both HF and POF3 simultaneously appear around 100 (cid:3)C and decrease at 170 (cid:3)C following a similar pattern might be explained by the fact that the residual amount of humidity was insufﬁcient to convert POF3 in HF

claims.claim: No vibrational bands for PF5 were observed during analysis because PF5 is likely too short-lived due to its instability and tendency to react with residual water or organic solvents.
claims.quote: No vibrational bands for PF5 were observed in the analysis, as it probably is too short-lived because of its instability and tendency to react with residual water or with the organic solvents

claims.claim: For about half of the working cells, for all levels of cycle ageing (0–300 cycles), ignition of accumulated battery-released gases occurred approximately 15 seconds after the thermal runaway, resulting in a gas explosion.
claims.quote: For about half of the working cells, for all levels of cycle ageing, ignition of the accumulated battery released gases occurred about 15 s after the thermal runaway resulting in a gas explosion.

claims.claim: Dead cells underwent thermal runaway but showed significantly higher thermal runaway temperatures, lower temperature rise rates, lower peak temperatures, and did not emit sparks, flames, fire, or cause gas explosions, compared to working cells.
claims.quote: The dead cells have a signiﬁcantly higher thermal runaway temperature, lower temperature rise rate and lower peak temperatures... the video analysis did not show any ﬂares/sparks/jet ﬂames, nor ﬁre or gas explosion occurred.

claims.claim: Toxic gas emissions of CO, HF, and POF3 were detected only in the third vent (simultaneous with thermal runaway), regardless of whether fire occurred or not, and were not detected in the first or second vents.
claims.quote: Gas emissions of CO, HF and POF3 were measured in all four tests where the FTIR gas measurement was employed, regardless if there was a ﬁre or not... HF and POF3 are not yet detected. The large cell opening of the cell safety vent occurs in the third vent, and this is the only vent phase where HF as well as POF3 are detected.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway temperature for working cells was approximately 190°C and showed a weak local minimum between 100 and 200 cycles within the tested range of 0–300 cycles, while dead cells had thermal runaway temperatures between 201 and 205°C.
claims.quote: The thermal runaway temperature was about 190 °C and was shown to have a weak correlation with the number of cycles in the ageing process, having a minimum value between 100 and 200 cycles, within the tested cycle range of 0–300 cycles... the thermal runaway temperature values for the working cells are about 190 °C, and range between 201 and 205 °C for the dead cells.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway temperature for the same cell type differed by approximately 50°C between oven external heating tests (approximately 190°C) and ARC test measurements (approximately 140°C), demonstrating that thermal runaway temperature values depend on the test method and its definition.
claims.quote: If the thermal runaway temperature is determined from the ARC measurement in a similar way as used in the oven experiment, the result is approximately 140 °C... The results for the two measurements thus diﬀer by about 50 °C. The thermal runaway temperature values for the same type of cell thus depend on the test method and on its deﬁnition.

claims.claim: During overcharge tests of a commercial LFP cylindrical 26650 cell at 2C rate, venting and thermal runaway were observed without explosion in seven out of nine reproducible tests, while in the other two tests the cell surface temperature rose to 300°C and 350°C respectively, leading to explosion.
claims.quote: During the seven reproducible overcharge tests, venting and thermal runaway were observed without explosion. During the other two, the cell surface temperature rose respectively to 300 °C and 350 °C, possibly caused by a strong internal short circuit, leading to an explosion that precluded gas analysis.

claims.claim: The first gas release from the LFP cell during overcharge occurred at a surface temperature of 53°C and a SOC of 120%, with detected species including DMC, EMC, CH4, CO, CH3OCHO, CH3OCH3, and CO2.
claims.quote: At 362s and as mentioned previously in section 3.1. (step D in Fig. 3b), a small gaseous production is observed. This occurs at a surface temperature of 53 °C and for a SOC of 120%. The detected molecules are: DMC, EMC, CH4, CO, CH3OCHO, CH3OCH3 and CO2.

claims.claim: HF formation during overcharge testing appeared only when all other gas species concentrations began to decrease, indicating that HF formation is decoupled from the other reaction mechanisms.
claims.quote: The HF formation mechanism appears to be decoupled from the other reaction mechanisms as HF appears when all the other gases start to decrease.

claims.claim: The amount of CO2 present in the vent gases released during overcharge of the LFP cell is not sufficient to prevent flammability, likely counterbalanced by the presence of flammable gases such as H2.
claims.quote: we showed that the amount of CO2 in the vent gases is thus not suﬃcient to prevent ﬂammability and is probably counterbalanced by the presence of ﬂammable gases such as H2.

claims.claim: The similarity in vent gas composition between overcharge abuse tests and thermal abuse tests on an overcharged LFP cell indicates that temperature, rather than oxidoreduction reactions, is the key parameter responsible for the main off-gas release.
claims.quote: The similarity in the vent gas composition obtained either in overcharge abuse tests or in thermal abuse tests on an overcharged cell indicates that oxidoreduction reactions seem to be poorly responsible for the main oﬀ-gas release by the cell whereas temperature is the key parameter.
